title: Add riscv64 to release artifacts and CI
body:
### Is this a feature request?

Yes — add `linux/riscv64` to the release artifact pipeline and CI.

### Description

Podman builds and runs successfully on native riscv64 hardware. Currently, release artifacts only include `linux_amd64` and `linux_arm64`. Adding riscv64 would enable container management on RISC-V boards and servers.

### Evidence

- **Hardware**: BananaPi F3 (SpacemiT K1, rv64gc, 8 cores @ 1.6 GHz, 16 GB RAM, Debian Trixie)
- **Go version**: go1.24.4 linux/riscv64
- **Build command**: `make binaries`
- **Build time**: ~18 minutes
- **Binaries produced**: podman (60MB), podman-remote (43MB), quadlet, rootlessport
- **Version**: `podman version 6.0.0-dev`

### CI Runners

Native riscv64 runners are available for free via [RISE RISC-V runners](https://github.com/apps/rise-risc-v-runners). Currently used by numpy, llama.cpp, pytorch, and Apache Arrow (in progress).

### Proposed change

Add `podman-remote-static-linux_riscv64.tar.gz` to `release-artifacts.yml` following the existing arm64 pattern:
```
make podman-remote-static-linux_riscv64
```

Note: this work is part of the [RISE Project](https://riseproject.dev/) effort to improve software ecosystem support on riscv64 platforms.

comments.body:
Validated on RISE native riscv64 runners: https://github.com/gounthar/podman/actions/runs/23318754735

Build time: **17 minutes** on native hardware (ubuntu-24.04-riscv). Podman 6.0.0-dev builds and runs.

Native runners are available for free via [RISE RISC-V runners](https://github.com/apps/rise-risc-v-runners) for CI integration.

I like the idea, but I think we need to discuss it with the other maintainers first. @containers/podman-maintainers

We may not be able to support this architecture given our limited hardware, but builds are okay on my end.

How do these build and run times numbers for riscv64, compare with the amd64 and arm64 times?

There were some complaints recently: https://marcin.juszkiewicz.com.pl/2026/03/10/risc-v-is-sloooow/

@Honny1 Thanks for considering it! Regarding hardware: RISE (riscv.org/rise) provides free native riscv64 GitHub Actions runners for open source projects. numpy, llama.cpp, and Apache Arrow are already using them. So no hardware investment needed from the Podman team.

@afbjorklund Fair question. Here are the numbers:

**Build times** (podman `make binaries`):
- BananaPi F3 (SpacemiT K1, 8 cores @ 1.6 GHz): 18 minutes
- RISE native runner (Scaleway EM-RV1): 17 minutes

I don't have amd64/arm64 comparison numbers for the exact same build, but for reference, the RISE runners are comparable to a low-end ARM server. The build would not block CI since it can run in parallel with the existing amd64/arm64 jobs.

Regarding Marcin's blog post: yes, current RISC-V hardware is slower than mature ARM/x86. But it's fast enough for CI builds (17 min is acceptable), and the goal is to have binaries ready when faster hardware ships. The SpacemiT K3 (RVA23, 16 cores) and SiFive P870 are coming soon with much better performance.

The PR (#28331) only adds the `podman-remote-static` binary to release artifacts. It cross-compiles on the existing CI runner, so it adds minimal overhead to the release process.

It's a nice proof of concept that there is a GitHub Action and it builds on riscv64. (Definitely, I don't want to run CI tests on ricsv64. It would "never" finish.) However, the actual solution for how it gets built could be different if we decide to go forward with it. For example, Go supports multi-architecture builds, but the resulting binary actually might not work due to library linking, etc. In an **ideal world**, you could cross-compile that binary on a fast amd64 machine.

We had the same situation with arm64 for a _long_ time, when I did the builds (and still do) on Raspberry Pi...

For riscv64 I just have the [StarFive VisionFive](https://www.waveshare.com/product/risc-v/boards-kits/visionfive2/visionfive2.htm), and it is mostly sitting there on the shelf next to the old RPi

---

Note: if you want to try it locally (in emulation), you can use: (with `lima-additional-guestagents` installed)

`limactl start --vm-type qemu --arch riscv64 template:podman`

OOPS, spoke too soon. Need to edit the template, `fedora-42`

`# No RISC-V release yet for Fedora 43: https://download.fedoraproject.org/pub/alt/risc-v/release/`

```console
$ podman.lima version
Client:        Podman Engine
Version:       5.8.0
API Version:   5.8.0
Go Version:    go1.26.0
Built:         Thu Feb 12 16:41:26 2026
Build Origin:  brew
OS/Arch:       darwin/arm64

Server:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.4.1
API Version:  5.4.1
Go Version:   go1.24rc1
Git Commit:   b79bc8afe796cba51dd906270a7e1056ccdfcf9e
Built:        Tue Mar 11 01:00:00 2025
OS/Arch:      linux/riscv64
```

Think the mirrors are not up-to-date either, while upgrading.

`Librepo error: Cannot download repomd.xml: Cannot download repodata/repomd.xml: All mirrors were tried`

----

EDIT: It seems like the mirror http://fedora.riscv.rocks is down.

And there are no official mirrors for riscv64, and no 43 release.

There are some builds being made however, if editing the repo.

```
[fedora-riscv-koji]
name=Fedora RISC-V $releasever - Koji
#baseurl=http://fedora.riscv.rocks/repos/f$releasever-staging/latest/$basearch/
baseurl=https://riscv-koji.fedoraproject.org/repos/f$releasever-build/latest/$basearch/
enabled=0
gpgcheck=0
priority=97
```
$ sudo dnf --enablerepo=fedora-riscv-koji install --best podman

```
Package                    Arch     Version                     Repository               Size
Upgrading:
 podman                    riscv64  5:5.6.2-1.fc42              fedora-riscv-koji    48.9 MiB
   replacing podman        riscv64  5:5.4.1-1.fc42              fedora               51.7 MiB
```

https://fedoramagazine.org/risc-v-and-fedora-all-aboard/
(https://discussion.fedoraproject.org/t/follow-up-fedora-43-risc-v-status/179519)
I think it'd be nice to enable a non-blocking `Alt Arch Riscv64` CI build job using the native runner before we get it into the release pipeline. @gounthar Is that something you could do a PR for?

I object this.

Adding yet another job nobody is paying attention to is not going to improve anything and just makes our CI maintenance harder. We use cirrus CI so any builds must happen there.

We already have cross builds to ensure podman compiles in cirrus so if we want riscv builds they should be done there not natively
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/0147339e8ef8da06182467367d986d67c3517349/contrib/cirrus/runner.sh#L283-L305

As pointed if a native build takes 17 mins that is just not acceptable for upstream contributors to wait for a single build that long and shows any actual functional testing will be impossible on that hardware as it would be way to slow.

IMO as upstream it is not our task to target each and ever platform. Practically speaking a cross build will catch most go compile issue already so that would seem good enough and the vast majority of PRs never do any architecture specific things so wasting time maintaining a completely separate CI system is not useful to me.

Wow, MIPS! Haven't seen that in a while.

> Hardware: BananaPi F3 (SpacemiT K1, rv64gc, 8 cores @ 1.6 GHz, 16 GB RAM, **Debian Trixie**)

Note also that there are already packages available here: https://packages.debian.org/trixie/podman

They are being built for "riscv64" arch too.

Yes AFAIK debian has working packages and fedora also has riscv64 SIG which seems to build podman fine I guess https://riscv-koji.fedoraproject.org/koji/packageinfo?packageID=564

As for the release artifacts we only offer podman-remote there which IMO is not useful and we seen plenty of confused users thinking that this is the real podman. Given podman is dynamically linked and depends on a lot of other things there is no point in providing static release binaries as such our answer has always been use your distro packages.
(And well if your distro does not support riscv then you have nothing to run on anyway)

> Wow, MIPS! Haven't seen that in a while.

IIRC these came from requests from the openwrt maintainers who do build podman on it

https://openwrt.org/docs/guide-user/virtualization/podman
https://github.com/openwrt/packages/blob/master/utils/podman/Makefile

Though seems like it has not been updated in a while

My last time was https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cobalt_RaQ (original v1)

Actually how I got started with containers, nee Virtual Private Servers

> Since podman already cross-compiles for several architectures in Cirrus CI, would a PR adding GOOS=linux GOARCH=riscv64 to the existing cross-build matrix in contrib/cirrus/runner.sh be welcome? That would validate compilation without any new CI system or native runners.

Yes lets do that, wou will need to add it to the main matrix in cirrus yaml https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/.cirrus.yml#L263-L264 and then add a new case in runner.sh to make it work. It seems certainly fair to have compile tests for riscv at this point.
